name: m16-gateup-threadcount-ab
purpose: Assess gate_up dual W4 GEMV kernel-time at the verify shape. Test the canonical 'fill the shape' lever (thread count 64 vs 128) via an env-gated launch-param probe; rocprof mtp_verifier (cached .so), W7900 gfx1100, chain-attn batched.

verify_gate_up.note: rows = tokens*top_k = 4*8 = 32; the real verify gate_up cost. Stayed WG=64 in both runs (the probe only reached the tokens=1 path); already at the tuned thread count.

threadcount_ab_tokens1.verdict: threads=128 neutral-to-worse (+1.5% median) AND splits the grid 8192->16384; the existing threads=64 tuning (since ca4796d8, May) holds.

kernel_quality: memory-bound W4 GEMV, already well-formed: coalesced transposed weight loads, 8-way vectorized FMA, shuffle-reduce, __restrict__, __launch_bounds__(128,4).
finding: No free kernel-time win for the gate_up GEMV. Thread count (the canonical fill-the-shape lever) is already tuned and 64->128 is neutral-to-worse. The kernel is memory-bound and well-optimized. The only remaining lever is output-tiling (MEGAKERNEL S9.3), which is a DISPATCH play (cheaper grid class + fewer x re-loads) with a per-block-serial tradeoff and B4 coalescing risk -- not a kernel-time win.
decision: DEFER a speculative output-tiling rewrite. The S0.1/S9.4 economics A/B proved verify kernel-time wins do not move C_B (dispatch-floored). C_B levers are the dispatch floor (S9.2) and multi-stream (S9.5). Do not regress an already-tuned, memory-bound GEMV.
